decks.title: Lecture 1 - Geometry, Equilibria, Boozer Coordinates, Metrics

slides.title: Stellarator optimization as a computational design loop
bullets: Four 90-minute lectures: from equilibrium objects to Pareto decisions
Default path: cached, honest, runnable; research path: optional live codes
slides.subtitle: Lecture 1: geometry, equilibria, Boozer coordinates, metrics

slides.title: Rogerio teaches the optimizer view of stellarator physics
bullets: Arturo: geometry and magnetic coordinates
Alessandro: neoclassical and turbulent transport
This block: how those quantities become objectives, constraints, and validation gates

slides.title: A modern design is a reproducible loop, not one optimized surface
bullets: equilibrium -> Boozer spectrum -> neoclassical metrics -> coils -> turbulence metrics -> profiles -> Pareto decisions
Each arrow creates a data artifact that must be checked and versioned

slides.title: The inverse problem starts with choices, not equations
bullets: Choose boundary variables, profiles, currents, and coils
Ask which physics model is trusted at this stage
Record the assumptions before minimizing a number

slides.title: The optimizer sees parameter vectors and diagnostics
bullets: Boundary Fourier modes
Pressure/current profiles
Equilibrium residuals
iota, aspect ratio, volume, beta, magnetic well

slides.title: Boundary Fourier modes are design variables with physics consequences
bullets: Small changes can modify symmetry, aspect ratio, and resonances at the same time
That coupling is why one-objective slides are misleading

slides.title: Profiles are part of the equilibrium object
bullets: Pressure and current profiles can change rotational transform and stability
A boundary-only story is a useful first approximation, not a full design

slides.title: VMEC is the first computational gate
bullets: Input: boundary and radial profiles
Output: nested-surface equilibrium and `wout` file
Teaching fallback: read public `wout` files or use labeled synthetic surfaces

slides.title: The `wout` file is a checked artifact
bullets: Inspect dimensions, variables, iota profile, aspect ratio, and surface geometry
Do not treat an equilibrium file as a black box

slides.title: HSX QHS is the compact live thread
bullets: Small stellarator case
Natural quasisymmetry example
Used across equilibrium, Boozer, and neoclassical metric notebooks

slides.title: Rotational transform is a profile, not a caption
bullets: It controls field-line pitch and resonance exposure
It can be targeted, constrained, or monitored during optimization

slides.title: Equilibrium failure is a teaching feature
bullets: Non-convergence is information
Bad resolution can produce attractive but false metrics
Cached mode lets the lecture continue while preserving the warning

slides.title: Notebook project: first equilibrium object
bullets: Load HSX `wout` if available
Plot LCFS and iota profile
Change one boundary-mode proxy and inspect metric coupling

slides.title: Boozer coordinates are where symmetry becomes measurable
bullets: Many design targets are statements about |B| in special coordinates
The spectrum separates symmetry-preserving and symmetry-breaking modes

slides.title: A Boozer contour gives a fast visual check
bullets: Clean bands suggest dominant intended symmetry
Damaged bands reveal off-symmetry content before reading a scalar residual

slides.title: A Boozer spectrum gives the optimizer a target
bullets: Dominant symmetry line: protect it
Off-line modes: penalize them
Weights encode physics judgment

slides.title: Quasisymmetry is not one number
bullets: QA, QH, and QP imply different mode structure
The chosen residual must match the intended symmetry family

slides.title: Quasi-isodynamic and omnigenous ideas enter as gates
bullets: Use enough theory to state the design target
Leave full derivations to the geometry and transport lectures

slides.title: A compact residual is useful only with a mode audit
bullets: Which modes were retained?
Which surface was used?
Was transform convergence checked?

slides.title: Notebook project: Boozer spectrum
bullets: Generate `B(theta,zeta)` from cached modes
Identify symmetry-breaking modes
Perturb one bad mode and watch the residual rise

slides.title: Neoclassical theory becomes an optimization metric through compression
bullets: Effective ripple summarizes a piece of low-collisionality transport risk
It is a screen, not a full transport solution

slides.title: Effective ripple compares configurations as profiles
bullets: Profile shape matters: edge and core can rank differently
A single scalar score hides where the risk lives

slides.title: Sensitivity matters more than the absolute cached value
bullets: A useful metric tells the optimizer which direction is worse
Finite differences are enough for the teaching version

slides.title: Metric hierarchy is a cost hierarchy
bullets: Geometry and Boozer metrics are cheap
NEO-like metrics are early transport screens
DKE/GK/profile calculations are validation gates

slides.title: Gradients are not a mathematical luxury
bullets: Finite differences are robust but expensive
Adjoints and autodiff make larger design spaces practical
Gradient quality must be checked against physics fidelity

slides.title: Autodiff changes the coding contract
bullets: Every operation in the objective must be differentiable or isolated
Cached mode teaches structure; research mode tests differentiability

slides.title: The scalar objective is a negotiation
bullets: J = magnetic quality + profile targets + coil risk + transport gates
Weights are values and risk choices, not constants of nature

slides.title: Never optimize a metric you cannot explain
bullets: Metric definition
Validation domain
Sensitivity direction
Known failure mode

slides.title: Data provenance is a scientific result
bullets: Real public HSX and W7-X files are fetched by script
Synthetic/cached arrays are labeled and documented in STATUS.md

slides.title: Computational checkpoint for lecture 1
bullets: Run environment check
Fetch equilibria
Generate figures
Open notebooks 01-03

slides.title: Exercise: change a boundary mode
bullets: Predict which metrics move first
Regenerate the surface and mode-scan figure
State what the toy model cannot prove

slides.title: Exercise: circle the bad Boozer modes
bullets: Which modes preserve the target symmetry?
Which modes would you penalize?
What happens if one bad mode triples?

slides.title: Common trap: clean plot, weak artifact
bullets: A nice contour is not enough
You need the input, mode truncation, surface choice, and convergence status

slides.title: Common trap: treating cached curves as results
bullets: Cached figures are educational objects
They keep the lecture reproducible
They do not replace verified code outputs

slides.title: Transition: a boundary is not a device
bullets: An optimized plasma boundary still needs coils
Stage-2 coil design can damage the stage-1 target

slides.title: Lecture 1 notebook map
bullets: 00 environment
01 equilibrium
02 Boozer spectrum
03 effective ripple

slides.title: Five rules from lecture 1
bullets: Use artifacts, not screenshots
Name the design variables
Plot profiles, not only scalars
State the validation domain
Keep cached mode honest

slides.title: Backup: when to switch to research mode
bullets: Package imports are not enough
Time a tiny run before class
Keep a cached plot ready for every live calculation

slides.title: Backup: what to inspect in a `wout` file
bullets: dimensions
iota
boundary coefficients
volume/aspect
beta and pressure-current profiles

slides.title: Backup: what to inspect in a Boozer file
bullets: surface labels
nfp
mode truncation
dominant and off-symmetry modes

slides.title: Backup: metric acceptance checklist
bullets: Does it move with the intended design variable?
Does it fail on known bad cases?
Does it correlate with a higher-fidelity gate?

slides.title: Appendix L1.42: discussion prompt
bullets: What would you trust in cached mode?
What would require a real-code run?
What failure mode should be documented?

slides.title: Appendix L1.43: discussion prompt
bullets: What would you trust in cached mode?
What would require a real-code run?
What failure mode should be documented?

slides.title: Appendix L1.44: discussion prompt
bullets: What would you trust in cached mode?
What would require a real-code run?
What failure mode should be documented?

slides.title: Lecture 1 close
bullets: A stellarator optimizer is only as good as its artifacts
Next: the plasma boundary has to be produced by coils

decks.title: Lecture 2 - Coils, Quadratic Flux, Single-Stage Optimization

slides.title: A plasma boundary is a wish; coils are the contract
bullets: Lecture 2 turns target fields into buildable magnetic hardware
The central metric is `B dot n`, but the constraints are engineering-heavy
slides.subtitle: Lecture 2: coils, stage-2 optimization, single-stage design

slides.title: Stage 1, stage 2, and single stage answer different questions
bullets: Stage 1: which plasma boundary is attractive?
Stage 2: which coils can make it?
Single stage: can plasma and coils improve together?

slides.title: The coil inverse problem is ill-posed
bullets: Many current distributions can approximate the same field
Regularization decides which solutions are buildable

slides.title: Quadratic flux is the basic stage-2 score
bullets: Measure normal-field error on the target surface
Low `B dot n` means the field respects the requested boundary

slides.title: Current-potential methods regularize a surface current
bullets: NESCOIL and REGCOIL logic: solve for currents on a winding surface
Regularization trades field accuracy for smoother current patterns

slides.title: Filamentary coils convert fields into curves
bullets: Curves have length, curvature, torsion, and separation
A field target can be easy for a surface current and hard for discrete coils

slides.title: SIMSOPT is the mature stage-2 teaching path
bullets: Geometry objects, curves, Biot-Savart fields, objectives, optimization loops
Research mode should use the verified public QA input

slides.title: Notebook project: SIMSOPT stage-2 coils
bullets: Load public QA input or cached coil fallback
Plot initial/final coils
Compare normal-field error before and after

slides.title: Initial coils show the geometry before optimization
bullets: The teaching curves are simple enough to inspect
The real lesson is the diagnostic grammar

slides.title: Final coils reduce field error but keep complexity visible
bullets: A coil plot without diagnostics is incomplete
Students should ask: length, curvature, clearance, ports

slides.title: Normal-field maps reveal what the coil picture hides
bullets: Before/after maps show where the target surface is damaged
Local hot spots matter for robustness

slides.title: Coil regularization is a Pareto problem
bullets: Shorter/smoother coils can worsen field accuracy
A single optimum hides a design choice

slides.title: Coil complexity has multiple dimensions
bullets: Length
Maximum curvature
Mean-squared curvature
Torsion
Coil-to-coil and coil-to-surface separation

slides.title: Ports and maintenance enter earlier than we wish
bullets: Access constraints can invalidate attractive coil sets
Use engineering constraints as penalties or hard gates

slides.title: HTS tapes add orientation and strain constraints
bullets: A reactor coil is not a mathematical filament
Curvature and force metrics must eventually become mechanical checks

slides.title: Fieldlines are coil diagnostics, not decoration
bullets: They reveal islands, stochasticity, and topology changes
They do not guarantee particle confinement

slides.title: Notebook project: ESSOS fieldlines and particles
bullets: Plot cached fieldline/orbit diagnostics
Use ESSOS in research mode only after timing
Discuss fast-particle relevance for reactors

slides.title: Fieldline tracing gives a topology sanity check
bullets: Good fieldlines support the coil story
Bad topology catches failures a scalar map can miss

slides.title: Particle confinement can fail after good fieldlines
bullets: Fast-ion and alpha-particle losses are reactor gates
Guiding-center/full-orbit fidelity should be chosen deliberately

slides.title: Two-stage optimization can lose the plasma target
bullets: Stage 1 may find a boundary no reasonable coils can make
Stage 2 may return coils that damage the original objective

slides.title: Single-stage optimization keeps coils in the loop
bullets: Boundary variables and coil variables move together
`J = J_plasma + w_flux J_flux + w_coil J_coil`

slides.title: Notebook project: single-stage toy
bullets: Plot objective pieces separately
Vary the flux penalty weight
Identify the penalty that prevents unbuildable solutions

slides.title: Objective histories expose hidden tradeoffs
bullets: The total objective can decrease while one term stalls
Plot terms, not just the sum

slides.title: Weight continuation is a design instrument
bullets: Start from an easier problem
Increase the coupling or constraint weight
Watch which term starts controlling the solution

slides.title: Direct coil optimization changes the gradient story
bullets: Biot-Savart fields are differentiable
Fieldline and particle objectives can be differentiated in JAX workflows
Gradient accuracy still needs validation

slides.title: Do not confuse differentiable with correct
bullets: Autodiff gives derivatives of the implemented model
The implemented model still has a validity domain

slides.title: Robustness is a stage-2 metric
bullets: Manufacturing errors
Current perturbations
Coil misalignment
Maintenance tolerances

slides.title: A coil objective needs units and scales
bullets: Length in meters
Curvature in inverse meters
Flux error normalized to field scale
Separation in centimeters

slides.title: Computational checkpoint for lecture 2
bullets: Run notebooks 04, 05, 06 in cached mode
Regenerate coil and fieldline figures
Record which paths are cached vs live

slides.title: Exercise: increase length penalty
bullets: What improves?
What worsens?
Which metric would you plot before choosing?

slides.title: Exercise: choose a coil set
bullets: Design A: lower `B dot n`, higher curvature
Design B: smoother, worse flux
Which one survives a first experiment?

slides.title: Common trap: beautiful coils without a target-surface map
bullets: A coil rendering can hide normal-field hot spots
Always pair coil geometry with `B dot n` or flux diagnostics

slides.title: Common trap: no separation metric
bullets: Coils can cross or approach each other in mathematical optimization
A physical design needs clearance constraints

slides.title: From coils to transport
bullets: A buildable field is not automatically a good confinement field
Next lecture: transport metrics become objective functions and gates

slides.title: Lecture 2 notebook map
bullets: 04 stage-2 coils
05 single-stage toy
06 fieldlines and particles
11 Pareto dashboard preview

slides.title: Five rules from lecture 2
bullets: Pair every boundary with coils
Plot `B dot n` maps
Keep coil complexity explicit
Use continuation for coupled objectives
Treat engineering as a gate

slides.title: Backup: stage-2 objective terms
bullets: quadratic flux
length
curvature
separation
linking/topology constraints

slides.title: Backup: SIMSOPT research path
bullets: Use public QA input
Start with a tiny replay
Do not run a long optimization live without cached fallback

slides.title: Backup: fieldline vs particle metrics
bullets: Fieldline: topology
Guiding-center: fast-ion screen
Full orbit: validation
Wall map: engineering risk

slides.title: Backup: single-stage acceptance checks
bullets: terms decrease together
no unbuildable coil geometry
target surface preserved
robustness not worse

bullets: What would you trust in cached mode?

bullets: What would you trust in cached mode?

bullets: What would you trust in cached mode?

bullets: What would you trust in cached mode?

slides.title: Lecture 2 close
bullets: Coils turn a magnetic design into a device claim
Next: transport physics tests whether the field is worth building

slides.title: Transport physics becomes optimization metrics
bullets: Lecture 3 treats neoclassical, turbulence, and fast particles as metrics and validation gates
The goal is not to rederive DKE or GK, but to use them responsibly
slides.subtitle: Lecture 3: neoclassical, turbulence, fast-particle metrics

slides.title: Use a cost hierarchy, not one favorite code
bullets: Geometry metrics are cheap
Boozer and effective-ripple metrics screen designs
DKE, gyrokinetics, particles, and profiles validate finalists

slides.title: Neoclassical recap for optimizers
bullets: Symmetry breaking produces trapped-particle radial drifts
Effective ripple compresses one transport risk
Ambipolar electric fields and bootstrap current need richer models

slides.title: Effective ripple is an early design screen
bullets: Useful: fast, profile-like, interpretable
Insufficient: does not replace a transport matrix or experiment

slides.title: NEO_JAX and SFINCS_JAX have different roles
bullets: NEO_JAX: compact effective-ripple diagnostics
SFINCS_JAX: richer radially local drift-kinetic validation

slides.title: Notebook project: SFINCS-JAX cached validation
bullets: Read a collisionality scan
Inspect Er roots
Plot a bootstrap-current proxy

slides.title: A D11-like scan teaches the validation shape
bullets: Transport coefficients change across regimes
Suppression mechanisms are profile and field dependent

slides.title: Ambipolar Er roots are not decorative
bullets: Multiple roots can imply different impurity and turbulence behavior
Optimization should not hide the root choice

slides.title: Bootstrap current is a constraint, not an afterthought
bullets: It changes equilibrium and island risk
It depends on profiles and collisionality

slides.title: W7-X shows why neoclassical optimization matters
bullets: Reduced neoclassical losses are a stellarator-design success
The next bottleneck can still be turbulence

slides.title: The turbulence question is a fidelity ladder
bullets: Linear growth: fast screen
Quasilinear weights: ranking hypothesis
Nonlinear heat flux: expensive validation
Profiles: performance closure

slides.title: Notebook project: SPECTRAX-GK linear metric
bullets: Plot growth rate vs `ky rho_i`
Plot mode frequency
Say what the proxy cannot prove

slides.title: Linear growth rates are useful but dangerous
bullets: They indicate instability drive
They do not determine nonlinear heat flux alone

slides.title: Frequency helps identify branches
bullets: Growth and frequency together are more informative than growth alone
Still not a confinement prediction

slides.title: Proxy optimization can pick the wrong design
bullets: A proxy may rank designs differently from nonlinear validation
The optimizer will exploit whatever metric you give it

slides.title: Notebook project: proxy vs validation
bullets: Choose the best design by each metric
Compare the names
Discuss why the rankings differ

slides.title: W7-X ion-temperature clamping motivates turbulence-aware design
bullets: Neoclassical success does not eliminate turbulent limits
Profile response can saturate performance

slides.title: Fast particles are reactor gates
bullets: Present experiments may tolerate losses that reactors cannot
Alpha confinement, wall loads, and orbit classes should enter late-stage validation

slides.title: Particle metrics connect back to coils
bullets: Coil errors can alter field topology
Fast-particle diagnostics should be rerun after coil design changes

slides.title: Metric trust ladder
bullets: screen
ranking hypothesis
validation calculation
experiment or integrated scenario

slides.title: Validation means comparing failure modes too
bullets: Does the metric fail on a known bad design?
Does it rank a known good design correctly?
Does it change smoothly under small perturbations?

slides.title: Computational checkpoint for lecture 3
bullets: Run notebooks 07, 08, 09
Record which curves are cached
Replace one cached column only if a real run is timed

slides.title: Exercise: choose early and late metrics
bullets: Which metric screens 1000 designs?
Which metric validates 5 finalists?
What is the failure mode of each?

slides.title: Exercise: proxy vs nonlinear ranking
bullets: Select the proxy optimum
Select the validation optimum
Explain why the optimizer might prefer the wrong one

slides.title: Common trap: treating a proxy as validation
bullets: A proxy is valuable precisely because it is incomplete
The deck must say what physics it omits

slides.title: Common trap: cost-free metrics
bullets: Every high-fidelity metric consumes time, expertise, and input hygiene
Budget fidelity across the design loop

slides.title: From transport to profiles
bullets: Transport coefficients are not the final output
Profiles and sources decide whether a field supports performance

slides.title: Lecture 3 notebook map
bullets: 03 effective ripple
07 neoclassical validation
08 linear GK metric
09 turbulence surrogate

slides.title: Five rules from lecture 3
bullets: Use cheap screens early
Validate with richer physics late
Plot profiles and regimes
Never hide proxy failure
Record the trust ladder

frequency

nonlinear heat flux
profile response

slides.title: Backup: fast-particle outputs to track
bullets: loss fraction
loss time
wall hit pattern
orbit class
sensitivity to coil errors

slides.title: Backup: when to trust cached mode
bullets: for teaching workflow
for plotting grammar
for exercises
not for design claims

slides.title: Lecture 3 close
bullets: Transport metrics are valuable because they are imperfect and staged
Next: close the loop with profiles and Pareto decisions

decks.title: Lecture 4 - Profiles, Pareto Fronts, Repo Lab

slides.title: From optimized field to credible design workflow
bullets: Lecture 4 integrates fields, coils, transport, profiles, and decisions
The deliverable is not a number; it is an auditable design argument
slides.subtitle: Lecture 4: profiles, Pareto fronts, repo lab

slides.title: The full loop has memory
bullets: Geometry changes transport
Transport changes profiles
Profiles change objectives
Coils and engineering constrain what survives

slides.title: Profiles are not optional
bullets: Temperature and density gradients drive transport
Sources and sinks close the performance claim

slides.title: NEOPAX or fallback profile closure
bullets: Research path: profile-oriented JAX workflows
Cached path: educational radial diffusion/source model

slides.title: Notebook project: profile closure
bullets: Plot Ti, Te, and source balance
Increase turbulent transport
Explain why field-only optimization is incomplete

slides.title: Profile evolution reveals transport consequences
bullets: Extra turbulent transport flattens the ion-temperature profile
A good field metric can still underperform after closure

slides.title: Power balance is a residual check
bullets: Sources, fluxes, and profiles must be mutually consistent
A profile plot without a balance check is weak evidence

slides.title: Integrated objectives should be staged
bullets: Early: cheap geometry and Boozer screens
Middle: coils and neoclassical metrics
Late: turbulence, particles, profiles, engineering

slides.title: Reactor constraints enter as gates
bullets: Alpha heating and losses
Wall loads and blankets
Tritium breeding
Maintenance and access
Materials and forces

slides.title: Coil robustness returns in the final workflow
bullets: Manufacturing tolerances
Current errors
Thermal/mechanical deformation
Field-error correction margin

slides.title: No single best design exists
bullets: Better transport can mean harder coils
Easier coils can mean worse turbulence or profiles
Decision weights must be explicit

slides.title: Notebook project: Pareto dashboard
bullets: Load cached design table
Compute nondominated points
Change weights and watch the selected design move

slides.title: Pareto fronts expose tradeoffs
bullets: Nondominated points are not all equally desirable
They are designs worth arguing about

slides.title: Weighted selection is a documented judgment
bullets: Weights encode priorities, risk tolerance, and program stage
Changing them should be visible and reproducible

slides.title: Validation gates prevent metric gaming
bullets: equilibrium
Boozer
neoclassical
coils
turbulence
particles
profiles
engineering

slides.title: The GitHub repo is the lab manual
bullets: Slides explain concepts
Notebooks execute projects
Scripts regenerate figures, movies, and status
STATUS.md records truthfulness

slides.title: Cached, tiny, and research modes serve different audiences
bullets: cached: student reliability
tiny: instructor live demo
research: real-code replacement after timing

slides.title: CI is part of teaching reproducibility
bullets: Cached-mode CI runs smoke tests, data fetch, figures, movies, notebooks, and pytest
Students can trust a green baseline before experimenting

slides.title: Notebook project: full repo run
bullets: `python scripts/check_release_ready.py`
`python scripts/execute_all_notebooks.py`
Review `STATUS.md` before making claims

slides.title: Design sprint: modify one objective
bullets: Choose one metric column
Change a weight or cached value
Regenerate one plot
Explain whether the design decision changed

slides.title: Design sprint: replace one cached artifact
bullets: Pick a real package output
Write it to `data/cached` with provenance
Update STATUS.md and rerun tests

slides.title: How students should use the repo after the school
bullets: Start cached
Change one notebook at a time
Keep generated outputs small
Document live-code failures honestly

slides.title: How researchers should use the repo
bullets: Replace fallback arrays with real outputs
Preserve notebook structure
Add tests for new artifacts
Avoid overclaiming new scientific results

slides.title: What belongs in git
bullets: small public inputs
small cached data
figures used by slides
notebooks and scripts
status and docs

slides.title: What does not belong in git
bullets: prompt-package PDFs/PPTX/zips
private screenshots
large generated runs
tokens or local environments

slides.title: Common trap: integrated workflow without ownership
bullets: Every artifact needs an owner: script, notebook, source, and status line
Otherwise the workflow cannot be taught or debugged

slides.title: Common trap: Pareto front as answer
bullets: A Pareto front is an argument surface
The final choice still needs context and validation

slides.title: Final design commandments
bullets: Do not show a boundary without coils
Do not show a metric without validation domain
Do not show a proxy without its failure mode
Do not show a Pareto point without the weights

slides.title: Commandment: keep cached mode honest
bullets: Label synthetic data
Do not fake `wout` files
Record limitations in STATUS.md

slides.title: Commandment: make every plot teach a decision
bullets: What changed?
Which design wins?
Which failure mode appears?
What would a higher-fidelity code check?

slides.title: Future of stellarator optimization
bullets: Coupled
Differentiable
Multi-fidelity
Profile-aware
Reproducible

slides.title: Repo lab: suggested 30-minute flow
bullets: 10 min environment and figures
10 min notebook edit
10 min Pareto decision discussion

slides.title: Backup: status report anatomy
bullets: package import status
data status
figure/movie status
notebook execution status
cached vs real demos

slides.title: Backup: live-demo abort criteria
bullets: unexpected GPU backend
long compilation
API mismatch
metric changes without time to explain

slides.title: Backup: next manual steps before school
bullets: Pick live demos
Build final slide exports
Run on projector machine
Prepare cached fallback narrative
